title: Baseline retest (no implementation change)
motivation:
Used by the orchestrator integration test (--from-hypothesis path).
Measures the unchanged baseline through the full eval pipeline so the
resulting log entry locks in the first fitness number for phase 8.
hypothesis:
No RTL changes — exercises the worktree -> emit_verilog -> formal ->
cosim -> fpga path on the existing rtl/*.sv to confirm the orchestrator
produces a structured log entry.

changes.description:
Sentinel — no real change. skip_implementation:true below bypasses
the implementation agent, so the file path here is metadata only;
it just has to satisfy the schema's rtl/<file> pattern. core.sv is
always present and untouched.

title: Move DIV/REM off the single-cycle ALU path
motivation:
The baseline ALU places RV32M multiplication and division in the same
combinational EX-stage result mux as common integer operations. A 32-bit
combinational divider is very likely to dominate LUT depth and area on
the Tang Nano 20K, yet CoreMark's timed region is matrix/list/state code
that is multiply-heavy and effectively division-free. Keeping DIV/REM
single-cycle optimizes an uncommon path while lowering Fmax for every
instruction.
hypothesis:
Replace the combinational DIV/DIVU/REM/REMU cases in rtl/alu.sv with a
small iterative RV32M divide/remainder unit and add an EX-stage interlock
only when one of those opcodes is in flight. Leave ADD/SUB/logic/shift,
branches, loads/stores, jumps, and MUL/MULH/MULHU/MULHSU on the current
single-cycle path. The timed CoreMark loop should pay no meaningful IPC
penalty because it does not execute DIV/REM, while the FPGA critical path
should shrink from divider-through-ALU-mux to the existing add/compare/mul
paths. Preserve exact RV32M divide-by-zero and INT_MIN/-1 semantics and
keep the RISCV_FORMAL_ALTOPS substitutions for formal builds.

changes.description:
Remove real combinational DIV/REM hardware from the normal ALU result
mux, retaining single-cycle non-divide ALU and MUL behavior plus the
existing ALTOPS formulas under RISCV_FORMAL_ALTOPS.

Add an iterative signed/unsigned divider/remainder block with start,
busy, done, result, and operation inputs, implementing all RV32M corner
cases before or after the unsigned divide iteration as appropriate.

Detect DIV/DIVU/REM/REMU in EX, start the iterative unit, hold the
ID/EX-to-EX/MEM advancement until done, then write the completed result
into the EX/MEM register with the original instruction metadata.

Add the divide-unit busy/done interlock to the existing stall network so
younger instructions, fetch, and RVFI retirement remain ordered while a
multi-cycle DIV/REM is completing.

Wire the new EX-stage divide stall signal into the top-level hazard
controls without changing the core IO shape or the single-retire RVFI
channel-0 convention.

title: Elastic Fetch Skid Buffer
motivation:
The baseline frontend has no real IF/ID storage: instruction data flows
directly from io_imemData into decode, and hazard_unit globally freezes the
PC on load-use, dmem stalls, and imem stalls. Under the tournament's ~22%
random imem backpressure, every not-ready fetch becomes a visible decode
bubble. Under dmem backpressure, the independent imem port is also idle even
though it could be fetching sequential instructions for after the stall.
CoreMark's IPC is already close to the single-issue limit, so the best
structural win is to recover wasted frontend cycles without widening the
core or lengthening the EX critical path.
hypothesis:
Replace the combinational IF/ID handoff with an elastic 2-entry fetch skid
buffer that decouples instruction fetch from decode. The fetch side advances
the PC and captures sequential instructions whenever io_imemReady is high
and the buffer is not full, even while ID/EX is held by a dmem stall. The
decode side consumes one buffered instruction per non-stalled cycle. Preserve
the baseline zero-latency behavior with an empty-buffer bypass from
io_imemData to decode when the backend is ready and imem is ready, so the
no-stall path and branch target refill latency do not regress. On EX
redirect, flush all queued entries and restart fetch at redirect_target.
This should hide a meaningful fraction of random imem stalls and avoid
wasting fetch bandwidth during backend stalls while keeping the core
single-issue, in-order, and RVFI channel-0-only.

changes.description:
Add a two-entry valid/ready fetch queue with a zero-latency bypass when
the queue is empty. Push fetched {pc,instr} entries on imemReady while
space is available, pop only when decode actually accepts the current
instruction, and flush the queue on reset or redirect.

Stop treating raw io_imemReady as a global pipeline stall. Keep load-use
and dmem interlocks, but let an empty fetch buffer naturally present
valid=0/NOP to ID while the fetch side waits for imemReady.

Wire the frontend consume/full/empty controls between if_stage and the
existing stall/flush network without changing the top-level core IO or
the NRET=2 RVFI port convention.

If needed, extend the IF/ID bundle or add small fetch-buffer typedefs for
queued pc/instruction/valid metadata; no architectural state or RVFI
field widths change.

implementation_notes:
# Elastic Fetch Skid Buffer

## What changed

- Reworked `rtl/if_stage.sv` into a two-entry elastic fetch skid buffer.
  - Empty-buffer bypass preserves the baseline zero-wait path from `io_imemData` to decode.
  - Fetch advances `io_imemAddr` when `io_imemReady` is high and the buffer has room, including while decode is blocked by load-use or dmem interlocks.
  - Decode pops one queued instruction only when the ID stage actually accepts the presented IF/ID item.
  - Reset, flush, and EX redirect clear queued entries; redirect restarts fetch at `redirect_target`.
- Updated `rtl/hazard_unit.sv` so raw `io_imemReady` no longer creates a global pipeline stall. The hazard unit now keeps only load-use and dmem interlocks.
- Updated `rtl/core.sv` wiring to pass `io_imemReady` into IF and remove it from the hazard unit.
- Added `rtl/000_core_pkg.sv`, an early-sorted guarded mirror of `core_pkg.sv`, so the required `verilator --lint-only -Wall -Wno-MULTITOP -sv rtl/*.sv` command sees typedefs/localparams before alphabetically later RTL consumers. Builds that explicitly read `core_pkg.sv` first see the mirror as a no-op through the shared `CORE_PKG_DEFINED` guard.

## Deviations

- I kept the existing five-stage topology and reused `stall_if` as the IF-side decode backpressure signal rather than adding new top-level internal control names.
- During a dmem stall with a redirecting instruction held in ID/EX, IF still treats the continuously asserted redirect as a squash. This preserves correctness but does not prefetch past that held redirect.

## Concerns

- `000_core_pkg.sv` duplicates declarations from `core_pkg.sv` to satisfy the fixed lint glob order. If `core_pkg.sv` changes later, the mirror must be kept in sync.

## Local status

- Lint: pass with `verilator --lint-only -Wall -Wno-MULTITOP -sv rtl/*.sv`.
- Formal: pass, 105 passed / 0 failed, run from a writable `/tmp/codex-formal-hyp-20260427-002-r1s1` copy because the worktree's `formal/riscv-formal` path is a symlink to a shared checkout outside the writable sandbox. A direct `bash formal/run_all.sh` in this sandbox failed before checks with `Operation not permitted` while clearing the staged core directory.
